scenarios.opening_query: The pet insurance we've paid into for six years denied our dog's surgery as 'pre-existing.'
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Insurance || General Litigation > Animal Law
scenarios.hidden_fact: The surgery already happened and he's fine — but the breeder knew about this congenital condition and hid the dam's history; the breeder-disclosure claim is the real recovery.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He's had the surgery and is fine — the breeder hid the congenital line, and that disclosure claim is the recovery.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether breeder concealment rather than coverage is the claim
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: No breeder issue — the insurer invented the 'pre-existing' label from a puppy checkup note; their denial practice is the fight.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No breeder issue — they built 'pre-existing' from a puppy checkup note.

scenarios.opening_query: A 'free government phone' kiosk signed my disabled uncle up for three paid plans he can't cancel.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > General || Administrative Law > Utilities
scenarios.hidden_fact: The plans hijacked his Lifeline subsidy — the telecom-benefit rules and the PUC complaint process govern getting his subsidized line back.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: They hijacked his Lifeline subsidy — the PUC complaint process is how his line comes back.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a regulated telecom benefit is at stake
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: No subsidy involved — straight deceptive sales to a vulnerable adult, with the carrier's cancellation stonewall as the target.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No subsidy — straight deceptive sales and a cancellation stonewall.

scenarios.opening_query: The at-home DNA kit company just emailed that my data was 'shared with research partners.'
scenarios.hidden_fact: The 'partner' turned out to be an insurance analytics firm — and my life-insurance renewal quote tripled two months later; the privacy tort with concrete damages is the case.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The 'partner' is an insurance analytics firm — and my life-insurance quote tripled two months later.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether downstream use caused concrete harm
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: No harm has landed — I want out of their ecosystem: deletion, contract cancellation, and the refund their terms promise.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No harm yet — I want deletion, cancellation, and the promised refund.
scenarios.vetting_note: Harm-realized privacy claim vs consumer exit.

scenarios.opening_query: My mother's hearing aids failed in warranty and the clinic that sold them closed permanently.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > General || General Litigation > Products Liability
scenarios.hidden_fact: The manufacturer's warranty runs with the device — and their national recall for this exact battery fault is active; the maker owes the remedy regardless of the dead clinic.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The manufacturer's warranty runs with the device and their recall covers this exact fault — the maker owes the remedy.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether manufacturer obligations survive the seller
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The maker's fine — it was the clinic's in-house 'extended service plan' we prepaid $1,800 for; recovering that from the closed business is the question.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The maker's fine — it's the $1,800 in-house service plan the closed clinic sold us.

scenarios.opening_query: A moving broker quoted $2,800 and the crew that showed up demands $9,400 before unloading.
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's an interstate move — federal hostage-load rules flatly prohibit this, and the federal complaint plus the carrier's broker-bond are the fast levers.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's interstate — federal hostage-load rules prohibit exactly this, and the federal complaint plus their bond are the levers.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's an in-town move — state consumer law and the sheriff's civil standby get my things off that truck.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: In-town move — state consumer law and a civil standby get my things back.

scenarios.opening_query: The 'estate sale company' we hired sold Grandma's things and never sent the family a dime.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > General || Wills & Trusts > Probate
scenarios.hidden_fact: The contract was signed by my aunt who had no authority — no personal representative existed yet; opening the estate and having the PR pursue the company (and maybe the aunt) is the proper channel.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: My aunt signed with no authority — no PR existed. Opening the estate and letting the PR pursue them is the channel.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether authority defects route recovery through the estate
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The estate's PR signed properly — the company just stole; the consumer-fraud and conversion case is direct.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The PR signed properly — the company just stole. Direct fraud case.
